title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
After returning from a long trip, Harold takes two pills from a pillbox provided by Alice and goes to bed. In a separate setting, Clyde expresses despondency to Miss Marple regarding the deaths of Alfred and Harold, as well as the poisoning of the entire family. Clyde reveals that the killer used aconite pills that were disguised to look identical to the victims' regular medication. The pillbox used was originally meant for Emma’s sleeping pills at Rutherford Manor, and while Emma’s fingerprints were on it, no other fingerprints were found.

Constable Craddock joins the discussion, expressing frustration over his inability to identify the killer or the identity of the dead woman. He notes that while he initially suspected Martina Crickenshaw, the victim was actually the wife of Sir Robert Stottard West. Miss Marple challenges the validity of a postcard from Jamaica used as evidence for Martina's whereabouts, suggesting it could be a fabrication. She also posits that the discovery of an envelope at Larchfield Manor—which contained a letter from Emma—might have been an intentional plant to divert the investigation away from Anna Stravinsky. Miss Marple suggests that someone impersonated Martina to manipulate money.

Later, Lucy visits Miss Marple and discusses the concept of a "pension," drawing a parallel to the Crickenshaw family will, which stipulates that if only one person remains alive, they inherit the entire substantial sum. Miss Marple attributes the escalating violence to human greed, noting that a desire for more can lead a person to commit murder.
